text: Hello friends, today we are going to present a laparoscopic right hemicolectomy for carcinoma cecum. This is a case of a forty-two year old female referred to us for further investigation for severe anemia. She had received injectable iron recently. We subjected her for upper GI endoscopy and colonoscopy. Upper GI endoscopy revealed normal. Coronoscopy revealed ulcerative proliferative growth at IC junction. It is just proximal to IC junction and this is the ulcerative proliferative growth. You can see here the narrow band imaging picture and then we have taken multiple biopsies from this particular growth. and send for histopathological reports.

Colonic biopsy revealed well-differentiated adenocarcinoma. The CT scan abdomen for this patient revealed focal thickening of cecum and ascending colon for a length of six point seven centimeters and it is fourteen mm in maximum thickness and there is an irregularity of underlying serosa and no significant luminal narrowing is detected. but there is no appendix is visualized on CT scan abdomen. There was no comorbidity, hemodynamically patient was stable, her hemoglobin was ten grams of gram percent and then she posted for laparoscopic right hemicolectomy.

The team who managed this case was Dr. Rahul Mahadhar myself, surgeon and endoscopist. Dr. A.M. Arora was assisting surgeon. Dr. Dhanashree Mahadhar was a camera surgeon. Dr. Mrs. Sonia Naik was anesthetist. And Dr. Nirmal Thakur was physician to manage this case. This was the operation theatre setup. Surgeon is standing on the left side of the patient. Camera holder is standing in between the legs of the patient. Leg split position is used and reverse tender bug position is used most of the times for this particular operation. T-tube monitors are on two sides of the patient at shoulder level. and the scrub nurse at the right leg side of the patient.

The laparoscopic setup for the right hemicolectomy includes a 10 mm port at the suprapubic region, a 12 mm port on the left side at the umbilicus level near the linea semilunaris, used for stapling, and a 5 mm port in the left subcostal region at the midclavicular line. A mirror image port is placed on the right side in the right subcostal region at the midclavicular line, and an additional port is positioned in the right iliac fossa.

The surgeon lifts the right mesocolon and performs a bottoms-up dissection between the mesocolon and Gerota's fascia. Facial dissection is completed, and vessels are taken. Supracolic dissection is performed around the hepatic flexure, and lateral attachments of the ascending colon and cecum are released, fully mobilizing the right side. An ileocolic side-to-side anastomosis is completed. A cystoscopic lighted ureteral stent is inserted on the right side. The patient is positioned in reverse Trendelenburg with legs split, and small bowel loops are shifted to the left side. The ileocolic vessel pedicle is visibly lifted.

The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to create a dissection plane between the ileocolic vessels and the mesocolon, extending towards the midline. The facial plane is dissected between the torus fascia and the gyrotos fascia. The ileocolic vein is visualized, and bleeding occurs, prompting the surgeon to focus on dissecting the ileocolic vessel.

The surgeon identifies the origin of the ileocolic vessel and applies hemoclips and a ligar clip. Using a ligature, the vessel is cut, and dissection is performed between the vessel and the artery. Lymphatic tissue is addressed at the artery's origin. The incision is extended laterally on the mesentery to create a large fascial plane between the right colon mesentery and the posterior fascia.

The surgeon dissects the vascular plane, extending it from lateral to medial, revealing the duodenum and fascia over it, which is managed using a ligature. The right ureter with a lighted stent is visible, covered by fascia, and above it lies the colon mesentery. The right ileocolic vessel is identified as it emerges directly from the aorta, and two hemoclips are applied to secure it.

The surgeon applies a lega clip and a hemo clip to the vessel, cutting it between the clips. Dissection is carried out laterally, revealing a thin papery peritoneal layer over the duodenum, which is taken down. A hole is made in the peritoneum, allowing further dissection along the side of the opening.

The surgeon continues the dissection cranially and laterally, carefully navigating the facial plane near the ureter and duodenum's lateral wall. The dissection progresses between the Gerota's fascia and the mesentery, with meticulous attention to small vessels to prevent bleeding and potential organ injury.

The surgeon uses a ligature to dissect the serotus fascia and the facial plane between the duodenum and the mesentery, reaching the upper border of the duodenum. The dissection is carried out further laterally under visualization.
The surgeon dissects the plane between the Gerota's fascia and the right side mesentery, clearly visible as an embryological plane. The dissection progresses as all fissures are carefully taken down.

The surgeon continues dissection laterally towards the pancreatic head, identifying small vessels in the area. The right branch of the middle colic vessel is ligated and cut using a ligature. Dense adhesions between the pancreatic head and surrounding structures are visible.

The surgeon uses a ligature to carefully dissect the mesentery, cutting steadily to avoid bleeding. Small vessels are managed with the ligature, and the gallbladder is visible from below. The dissection progresses around the bowel, addressing attachments near the pancreatic head. The supracolic compartment is reached, and small attachments are meticulously handled.

The surgeon completes the dissection of the right colon, addressing adhesions between the gallbladder and mesentery, and the liver bed using a ligature. The hepatic flexure is fully mobilized and dissected. Dissection proceeds on the lateral side at the cecum, with lateral attachments cut using a harmonic scalpel. The right ureter is visible as a glistening structure, with a catheter in place, while the harmonic scalpel is used to sever the remaining lateral attachments, completing the mobilization of the right colon.

The surgeon visualizes the kidney, ureter, and duodenum while addressing the terminal ileum mesentery using a ligature. The mesentery is dissected to reach the bowel wall, with fatty tissue visible around the area. The transverse colon is prepared for resection, and small mesenteric attachments are managed using a ligature. ICG mapping is performed to prepare the site for resection.

The surgeon completes the preparation for resection at the transverse colon site, administering 1 ml of ICG dye to map vascularity. The dye flow reveals the line of demarcation, indicating the area for resection. The surgeon uses a 60 mm purple Endo GIA stapler to transect the transverse colon.

The surgeon compares the two ends of the transected transverse colon, noting one end with complete vascularity and the other with no vascularity. The small bowel shows congestion at the cut portion, while the rest appears normal. ICG mapping reveals vascularity on the left side and lack of vascularity on the right side. The level of transection is determined based on vascularity, and the endo-GIA stapler, purple, is applied with slight angulation and fired to complete the transection.

The specimen is placed on the right side of the patient. The small bowel is mobilized for a side-to-side ileocolic anastomosis. Stay sutures are placed at the transected ends of the ileum and transverse colon. An Endo GIA 60 mm purple stapler is used for the anastomosis. An enterotomy is made on both the small bowel and transverse colon sides with a hook. The stapler is introduced, approximated, and fired. The staple line is inspected for hemostasis, confirming no bleeding. The enterotomy is closed with a U-Zero V-Lock barb suture, completing the first layer closure.

The surgeon completes full-thickness closure of the anastomosis site using sutures, followed by a second layer of suturing with the same material. The rent between the middle of the colon and the small bowel mesentery is closed to prevent internal herniation.

Continuous sutures are used to close the range, followed by ICG mapping to assess the vascularity of the anastomotic site between the transverse colon and small bowel. The vascularity appears well-maintained on both sides, indicating a stable anastomosis. A suprapubic transverse incision of approximately four centimeters is made, and a wound protector is introduced to facilitate the delivery of the specimen through the incision.

And then this wound is closed in layers. The total operation time for this particular operation was approximately eighty-eight minutes. And this is the growth at the cecum. And started orals on third postoperative day, postoperative period was uneventful, discharged on fourth postoperative day. The histopathology report turned out to be nineteen nodes dissected. all free of tumors and the histopathology will suggest you have well-differentiated adenocarcinoma with T-N-O-M-O.
Thanks for watching. Thank you.
